question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply an arbitrarily large blockTimestamp value to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to retrieve contract storage state that was never meant to be visible at that point in Hashgraph history, effectively reading future-anchored or out-of-consensus state and causing incorrect EVM execution results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass blockTimestamp=0 or a negative long value to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to cause the underlying SQL query (consensus_timestamp <= ?3) to return no rows or an unexpected row, effectively erasing visible Hashgraph history for a contract slot and causing EVM simulation to use a zero/empty value instead of the correct historical value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply blockTimestamp=Long.MAX_VALUE (9223372036854775807) to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to bypass any intended block range restrictions, causing the query to return the absolute latest contract_state_change record regardless of the intended historical block boundary, thereby manipulating the apparent Hashgraph history seen by the EVM?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a blockTimestamp that exactly matches a consensus_timestamp of a known transaction in contract_state_change to force the coalesce(value_written, value_read) expression to return value_read instead of value_written, thereby presenting a tampered view of what the contract storage contained at that Hashgraph consensus point?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the coalesce(value_written, value_read) fallback in the findStorageByBlockTimestamp() SQL query to retrieve value_read (the pre-transaction storage value) instead of value_written (the post-transaction storage value) by targeting a blockTimestamp where value_written is NULL, causing the EVM to simulate against a historically incorrect contract state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a slotKeyByteArray with all-zero bytes to findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to probe whether slot 0x0000...0000 has any historical entries in contract_state_change, and if the slot was never written, receive an empty Optional that causes the EVM to treat the slot as zero — potentially misrepresenting the Hashgraph-recorded initial state of the contract?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a slotKeyByteArray shorter than 32 bytes to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing the SQL query to perform a bytea comparison against a truncated key that may match a different slot in contract_state_change, thereby reading historical state belonging to a different storage slot and corrupting the EVM's view of Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a slotKeyByteArray longer than 32 bytes to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing the SQL bytea equality check to fail to match any legitimate slot in contract_state_change, effectively hiding the true historical value of that slot and causing the EVM to simulate with a zero value instead of the Hashgraph-recorded value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any upper-bound validation on blockTimestamp in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to query contract state at a timestamp beyond the latest ingested Hashgraph block, causing the mirror node to serve speculative or incomplete state as if it were confirmed Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user repeatedly call findStorageByBlockTimestamp() with incrementing blockTimestamp values to enumerate all historical state transitions of a contract slot stored in contract_state_change, effectively reconstructing the full Hashgraph transaction history of a contract's storage without any rate limiting or access control, enabling targeted manipulation of simulations at specific historical points?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a blockTimestamp that falls between two consecutive consensus_timestamps in contract_state_change to cause the query (consensus_timestamp <= ?3 order by consensus_timestamp desc limit 1) to return the older of the two records, thereby presenting a stale historical state that does not reflect the most recent Hashgraph-confirmed write at that block boundary?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() queries contract_state_change (historical table) while findStorage() queries contract_state (current table) to craft a scenario where the two tables are temporarily inconsistent during ingestion, causing a historical query at a near-present timestamp to return a different value than the current state query, effectively presenting a forked view of Hashgraph history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a null slotKeyByteArray to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing a NullPointerException or SQL error that leaks internal database schema information about the contract_state_change table structure, which could be used to craft more precise Hashgraph history manipulation attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user pass a null EntityId to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing entityId.getId() to throw a NullPointerException that bypasses normal query execution and results in an empty Optional being returned, causing the EVM to treat a contract's historical slot as zero when the Hashgraph history actually contains a non-zero value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user supply a fabricated EntityId with a contract_id that does not exist in the contract_state_change table to findStorageByBlockTimestamp(), causing the query to return an empty Optional and the EVM to simulate the contract as having zero storage at that historical timestamp, misrepresenting the Hashgraph-recorded state of a valid contract?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of caching in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() (unlike findStorage() which uses @Cacheable) to perform high-frequency historical timestamp scanning attacks against the contract_state_change table, causing database exhaustion and denial of service that prevents legitimate Hashgraph history queries from completing?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the consensus_timestamp <= ?3 boundary condition in findStorageByBlockTimestamp() to query at a timestamp that is exactly equal to a migration record's consensus_timestamp, causing the coalesce(value_written, value_read) to return a migration-era value that was never part of normal Hashgraph consensus, thereby injecting a pre-migration state into EVM simulation?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageByBlockTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that contract_state_change stores both migration=true and migration=false records, and that findStorageByBlockTimestamp() does not filter by migration flag, to retrieve a migration-era storage value at a blockTimestamp that predates the contract's actual deployment on Hashgraph, causing the EVM to simulate against a historically impossible state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the Caffeine cache in findStorage() to poison the contractStateCache with a stale value by triggering a cache population at a moment when the contract_state table is being updated by the importer, causing subsequent historical simulations that fall back to findStorage() (via the isKeyEvictedFromCache path) to use a tampered cached value instead of the true Hashgraph-recorded state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the EMPTY_VALUE sentinel (new byte[0]) in findStorage() to distinguish between a slot that was never written (returns empty Optional) and a slot that was written with an empty value (returns EMPTY_VALUE), and use this distinction to probe the Hashgraph history of a contract's storage layout without triggering any access controls?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorage()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the condition `cachedValue != null && cachedValue != EMPTY_VALUE` in findStorage() to cause a cache hit on EMPTY_VALUE to fall through to findStorageBatch(), which then queries the database and potentially returns a different (non-empty) value, creating an inconsistency between what the cache records as Hashgraph history and what the database actually contains?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger findStorageBatch() with a contractId that has a very large number of cached slot keys in contractSlotsCache, causing the findStorageBatch() SQL query to include thousands of slots in the IN clause, leading to a database performance degradation that delays ingestion of new Hashgraph blocks and creates a window where historical state queries return incomplete data?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the race condition in findStorageBatch() where contractSlotsCache.putIfAbsent(wrappedKey, EMPTY_VALUE) is called before the batch query executes, causing a concurrent thread to read EMPTY_VALUE from the cache for a slot that actually has a non-zero value in Hashgraph history, resulting in incorrect historical state being served to EVM simulations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the isKeyEvictedFromCache fallback in findStorageBatch() — where an evicted key causes a fallback to contractStateRepository.findStorage() (current state) instead of historical state — to cause a historical EVM simulation to use the current contract_state value rather than the contract_state_change value at the requested blockTimestamp, effectively substituting present Hashgraph state for historical Hashgraph state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/service/ContractStateService.java -> Scope: Critical: Any impact caused by Tampering/Manipulating Hashgraph history'] [Function: findStorageBatch()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a sequence of requests that causes the contractSlotsCache for a target contract to fill up and evict the target slot key, then immediately request that slot, triggering the isKeyEvictedFromCache=true path that falls back to findStorage() (current state query), causing a historical simulation to return the current Hashgraph state instead of the historically correct state at the requested blockTimestamp?
